Konsoer Lab · LSU · Geomorphology & Remote Sensing

How Water, Plants, and Sandbars Shape Louisiana's River Delta

Two studies from Dr. Kory Konsoer's research group investigate how the Mississippi River's landscape is changing — from tracking plants that disappear underwater with the tides using satellite imagery, to understanding how hidden sandbars in river channels redirect water flow and shape the delta's future.

2019–24
Satellite Time Series

+64%
More Flow to Side Channels
🌊

Dr. Kory Konsoer

Principal Investigator — River Geomorphology Lab

Dr. Konsoer's group studies how rivers shape their landscapes — how water flows through channels, how sediment moves, and how vegetation interacts with river processes. The lab uses a combination of satellite remote sensing, field measurements, and computer modeling to understand the physical geography of river deltas, with a particular focus on the Mississippi River system and its relevance to coastal land loss in Louisiana.

Department of Geography & Anthropology · Louisiana State University · Baton Rouge, LA
K1

Mapping Plants That Disappear With the Tides

● Project 1 — Submersible Vegetation Mapping

Some Plants Live Between Two Worlds

Walk along Louisiana's coastal waterways at low tide and you'll find lush green vegetation blanketing the shallow areas. Return at high tide and it's gone — swallowed beneath the water. These "submersible" plants grow in zones where water levels rise and fall enough to regularly cover them completely.

Until now, there was no reliable way to map where these plants are across large areas. That matters because submersible vegetation slows water flow, traps sediment, and stabilizes land — and as sea levels rise, more marsh areas may shift into this zone where plants spend part of their time underwater.

Submersible vegetation along the water's edge in South Pass — at low water fully visible, at high water completely submerged · July 2024
Short aquatic vegetation near South Pass, Mississippi River Delta — the green patches at the water surface are the plants this study tracked from space
~30%
of vegetation is submersible
Up to 30% of all plant cover in the study area can disappear beneath the water surface during high water events
30.46 km²
study area
South Pass of the Mississippi River Delta — a complex network of channels, marshes, and shallow water habitats
R² = 0.975
water level correlation
Near-perfect relationship between water level change and how much vegetation goes underwater — the bigger the water swing, the more submerges
K1.1

How Did Researchers Find These Plants From Space?

Step 01
🛰️
Collect Satellite Images
Select paired Sentinel-2 and PlanetScope images taken within days of each other — one at low water, one at high water — at the same location over 2019–2024
→
Step 02
📡
Match Water Level Data
Compare each image date against hourly water level records from CRMS station 0157 to know precisely how high the water was when each satellite photo was taken
→
Step 03
🌿
Map Vegetation in Both
Measure plant coverage in the low-water image (more plants visible) and the high-water image (fewer plants visible) using spectral vegetation indices
→
Step 04
📍
Find What's Submersible
Vegetation present at low water but absent at high water = submersible. These areas are mapped across the full 30 km² study zone for each year from 2020–2024
Study area in South Pass (b: full satellite view with CRMS 0157 station marked; c1: the same spot at high water — bare mud; c2: the same spot at low water — vegetation visible). This before/after comparison is the core of the mapping method.
K1.2

Five Years of Change — Interactive Map Explorer

How the Distribution Has Shifted Year to Year

The submersible vegetation maps for 2020 through 2024 reveal that while these plants consistently appear in the same general zones — channel edges, shallow interior areas, and junction points — the total extent and spatial pattern changes noticeably from year to year, closely tracking annual variations in water level fluctuation. Use the explorer below to step through each year or let it play automatically.

Submersible Vegetation Distribution · South Pass, MRD
Sentinel-2 & PlanetScope · 2020 – 2024

Area Mapped 2.37 km²

2023
4.18 km²
2024
4.15 km²
Year Notes
Relatively sparse distribution — primarily along western channel banks and southern margins. Lower water level fluctuation (ΔWSE ~0.5 ft) limits submersion extent.
The bigger the seasonal water level swing (ΔWSE), the more vegetation gets submerged. R² = 0.975 — an almost perfect correlation across all five years.
📈
2023–24 Were Peak Years
The largest extents (4.18 and 4.15 km²) were recorded in 2023 and 2024, coinciding with larger seasonal water level fluctuations. 2020 had the smallest mapped area at 2.37 km² during a year of lower water swings.
🗺️
Same Zones, Shifting Patches
While the core locations of submersible vegetation remain consistent across years — channel junctions, low-lying margins, shallow river interiors — the density and connectivity of patches shifts noticeably, reflecting year-to-year hydrological variation.
🌊
A Tool for Monitoring Change
This approach can now be applied annually using freely available satellite data — providing a repeatable way to track how submersible plant cover shifts over time as sea levels rise and the delta continues to evolve.
K2

How Hidden Sandbars Change Where the River Flows

● Project 2 — Channel Bifurcations & Shallow Bench Hydrodynamics

Where a River Splits in Two — and Why It Matters

As the Mississippi River approaches the Gulf of Mexico, it repeatedly splits into smaller channels — like a road branching into two paths. These split points control how much water, sediment, and nutrients flow into each branch, directly determining which parts of the delta receive enough material to keep building land.

Some of these splits have shallow underwater platforms — benches — that sit just below or at the water surface on one side of the channel, often covered by seasonal vegetation including Roseau cane and elephant ear. This study asked: how much do these benches actually change the way water flows through the split?

Study location in South Pass of the Mississippi River Delta. The close-up (right) shows the bifurcation — where the main channel splits, with a shallow bench visible on the right bank, photographed in 2023.
+64%
more flow to side channel
A bench at the bifurcation redirects 64% more water into the lateral branch compared to a flat riverbed with no bench
+6%
added by vegetation
Roseau cane on a submerged bench pushes an additional 6% more discharge into the lateral channel vs. a bare submerged bench
4
modeled scenarios
No bench · emergent bench · submerged bench · vegetated submerged bench — tested with the Delft3D hydrodynamic model
K2.1

Measuring the River — In the Field and in the Computer

Field Measurements With an ADCP

Researchers used an Acoustic Doppler Current Profiler (ADCP) — an underwater sonar device towed by boat that measures water velocity at multiple depths — to map flow at cross-sections of the bifurcation. Two surveys: December 2024 (low winter flow) and June 2025 (higher summer flows).

How an ADCP Works

It sends sound pulses downward. The pulses bounce off sediment particles in the water, and by measuring how quickly the echoes return at different depths, it calculates the speed and direction of the water throughout the entire water column in real time.

Computer Model — Four Scenarios

A Delft3D hydrodynamic model was built representing four configurations to isolate what each feature contributes to flow redistribution:

I No bench — flat riverbed baseline
II Emergent bench — platform above water surface
III Submerged bench — platform below water, no plants
IV Submerged bench — platform below water + Roseau cane
Survey I · December 2024 (low flow) — colors show water velocity: blue = slow, green/yellow = faster. The bench side shows distinctly different flow patterns from the open channel.
Survey II · June 2025 (higher flow) — covers both bifurcations. Red/orange areas = high-velocity main channel cores. The bench side creates a contrasting slow-flow zone.
K2.2

What the Model and Measurements Revealed

Without a bench — flow is relatively uniform (mostly green). Very little water diverts into the lateral branch (right, mostly slow blue).
With a submerged bench — dramatic velocity contrast (red/orange = fast, blue = slow near bench). High-momentum flow shifts away from the bench, more water enters the lateral channel.

How Much More Water Goes Into the Side Channel?

The model quantifies how each bench condition changes the split of water between the main and lateral channels:

ScenarioDescriptionMain ChannelLateral Channel
INo bench95.9%4.1%
IIEmergent bench (above water)93.3%6.7%
IIISubmerged bench, no vegetation92.7%7.3%
IVSubmerged bench + Roseau cane92.2%7.8% ↑
The Key Number

A vegetated bench nearly doubles the fraction of water entering the lateral channel (4.1% → 7.8%). Across an entire delta network of dozens of these splits, this consistent redirection determines which arms of the delta grow and which shrink.

Orange bars show the lateral channel's growing share of flow across scenarios I–IV. Each scenario reflects conditions that exist across real bifurcations in the delta.
🏗️
Benches Fundamentally Reshape Flow
Even a completely submerged bench continues to significantly affect water movement — creating velocity contrasts, secondary circulation, and wake zones that would not exist without it. Models that ignore these features miss a key part of delta physics.
🌱
As Roseau Cane Dies, Flow Patterns Shift
With Roseau cane dying back across the MRD and being replaced by seasonal elephant ear, the hydraulic character of bench surfaces is changing — with real consequences for where sediment accumulates and where land is built or eroded.
🗺️
Better Models for Coastal Restoration
These results provide data to improve predictive models used in Louisiana coastal restoration planning — helping engineers and managers better anticipate how land loss and gain will unfold under different restoration scenarios.

How These Two Projects Connect

Both studies focus on the South Pass of the Mississippi River Delta, and both reveal how features that are easy to overlook — plants that hide underwater, sandbars just below the surface — have outsized effects on how the landscape works. Together they show that the delta's future depends on understanding these subtle, smaller-scale processes that shape where water flows, where sediment lands, and where land is built or lost.

💰
Research Group
Both projects are part of the ongoing work of the Konsoer River Geomorphology Lab at Louisiana State University, Department of Geography & Anthropology. Research is conducted in collaboration with the TiGers Lab (AP23PPQS&T00C161) and utilizes data from the Louisiana Coastwide Reference Monitoring System (CRMS), PlanetScope and Sentinel-2 satellite platforms, and ADCP field instrumentation.
